nativeRange.habitat: Clear seaward reefs and drop-offs, typically 2–50 m depth

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on algae from reef surfaces, including algae growing on sponges. Generally advised to offer a varied diet of marine-based seaweed, dried nori, spirulina-enriched foods, and macroalgae at least 3 times per week. Will also accept mysis and other meaty foods, but a predominantly plant-based diet tends to support immune health and reduce aggression.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3732x2133; sharp, vivid full lateral view showing diagnostic fine orange body lines, yellowish dorsal band, blue-lined face, pale caudal peduncle and lyre tail; best overall.
Kai Squires; 2048x1537; sharp full-body of a different individual near substrate, clear blue face lines and orange flank; good variety.
Rickard Zerpe; 5184x3888; high-res browner adult color form, shows pale caudal peduncle, dark scalpel socket and yellow-tipped lyre tail; adds color variation.

summary: The Fowleri Tang is a large surgeonfish from Western Pacific reefs, generally considered reef-safe and valued for algae control. Best suited to very large, well-established aquariums, it tends to be semi-aggressive toward other surgeonfish and similar-shaped species.
careNotes: The Fowleri Tang (Acanthurus fowleri) is a large surgeonfish reaching around 45–50 cm, requiring a very large aquarium — generally advised to be at least 1500 L (approximately 400 US gallons) to provide adequate swimming room and territory. In the wild this species tends to be solitary and wary, grazing algae from reef surfaces. In captivity it tends to be semi-aggressive toward other surgeonfish and similar-shaped tankmates, while remaining largely peaceful toward unrelated species. Keeping only one individual per tank is generally advised unless an exceptionally large system is available. A well-established aquarium with abundant live rock is recommended to provide algae for natural grazing and territory within the rockwork. Diet should be heavily plant-based — nori, macroalgae, and spirulina-enriched foods — with meaty foods offered as a supplement. Juveniles display distinct coloration from adults and are sometimes not recognized as the same species in the trade. As with all members of the family Acanthuridae, this species possesses a sharp, blade-like spine at the base of the tail (known as a caudal scalpel) that can inflict painful wounds during handling or netting; care is advised when working in the tank. Highly oxygenated water with moderate to high flow has been reported to be beneficial.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented in the hobbyist literature. No dedicated captive-breeding program appears to exist for this species at time of research.
